text.heading: CBN to banks: disclose stake in Transcorp, Virgin
text.p: From the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) yesterday came a directive to all banks and discount houses: submit details of your exposures including commercial papers (CPs) and Bankers Acceptances to Transnational Corporation Plc (Transcorp) and Virgin Nigeria Limited (VNL).
Besides, the directive contained in a June 15 circular requested for "sold and bought counter-parties" in respect of both companies.
The circular signed by CBN’s Acting Director, Banking supervision, Dan Eke, gave the banks up till June 19 to comply with the directive.
A bankers’ acceptance is created when a time draft drawn on a bank, usually to finance shipment or temporary storage of goods, is stamped "accepted" by the bank.
By accepting the draft, the bank makes an unconditional promise to pay the holder of the draft a stated amount at a specified date.
Acceptances are traded at a discount from face value on the secondary market. Banker’s acceptances are similar to Treasury-bills and are often used in money market funds.

Commercial Papers are unsecured, short-term debt instruments issued by banks for the financing of accounts, receivable, inventories and meeting short-term liabilities. Commercial paper is not usually backed by any form of collateral, so only firms with high-quality debt ratings will easily find buyers without having to offer a substantial discount (higher cost) for the debt issue.

On June 1, the National Council on Privatisation (NCP) revoked the sale of Nigerian Telecommunications (Nitel) and Mobile Telecommunications (Mtel) to Transcorp. Transcorp had acquired the firms for $500m in August 2006.
Transcorp’s acquisition of NITEL/Mtel stirred controversy in the land.
The workers kicked against the transaction, claiming that it was against the nation’s interest.
Many even perceived that Transcorp acted as a front for former President Olusegun Obasanjo to buy the public utility.
Their suspicion was fuelled by Obasanjo’s claudestine investment of N200million in Transcorp through a blind trust.
During the period Transcorp that ran NITEL/Mtel, it neither pay staff salary for 13 months nor settle pensioners’ claim.
A few weeks ago, Transcorp Managing Director/Chief Executive Tom Iseghohi was arrested over what many believe is not unconnected with the NITEL/Mtel crisis.
An Abuja Federal High Court has ordered that he be remanded in custody.
The deal between the government and Virgin Atlantic seems to have gone awry, with the planned change in the name of Virgin Nigeria.

Hinting of the planned change in Lagos about 10 days ago, Virgin Nigeria Managing Director Captain Dapo Olumide said since its inception in 2005, the company’s staff had been undergoing training with Virgin Atlantic.
"They have taught us how to work. Nigerians have acquired those skills and we are going to put them to work. We have done it before and we are going to do it again.
"Virgin Atlantic cannot be here forever. The longer Virgin Nigeria remains under the umbrella of Virgin Atlantic, the longer it will take us to chart our own identity".
"It is an interesting turning point … it is like the parent is now letting the child go off on its own," an executive with Virgin Nigeria, who pleaded for anonymity, told Reuters.
